coredata.dc:title: Catalyst regulation of o-phenylenediamine-based carbon dots to achieve single red emission
coredata.dc:creator: He, Pinyi
Bai, Jianliang
Qin, Fu
Wang, Xinyu
Yu, Xu
Yao, Yongkang
Ren, Lili
coredata.dc:description: Red fluorescence is of great significance in biomedicine because of its high tissue penetration and low photodamage to organisms. While the carbon dots (CDs) obtained from o-phenylenediamine (oPD) mainly emit yellow fluorescence in a single reaction system, which greatly limits its development in long-wavelength applications. Here, we design a strategy to control the color of oPD-based CDs by only adding catalyst without changing the materials and conditions. The addition of the catalyst made the mass proportion of the obtained red CDs from less than 1% to 95.6%, and the conversion rate was up to 61.7%. The red CDs with a photoluminescence quantum yield of 29.99% have been successfully applied to live cell imaging. It is well known that catalysts can selectively change the reaction pathway and improve the reaction activity. In the process of oPD-based CD formation, we propose that the catalyst can activate the amino functional group, further accelerate the dehydrogenation and polymerization process, thus effectively increasing the sp2-conjugated domain and graphitic N content, which finally realizes the conversion of mixed CDs to single red CDs. This work provides a new idea for how to regulate the fluorescence of CDs without changing the materials and conditions in the future.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Catalyst
Red emission
Luminescence regulation

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We gratefully acknowledge the support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 22275035 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Photographs of four kinds of red CDs separated by silica gel column chromatography. The corresponding proportions of red CDs were: less than 1% (oPD-CDs), 95.6% (AlCl3-CDs), 21% (CuCl2-CDs), and 19% (FeCl3-CDs).

TEM and HRTEM images (inset) of (a) oPD-CDs, (b) AlCl3-CDs, (c) CuCl2-CDs, and (d) FeCl3-CDs. Particle size distribution diagrams of (e) oPD-CDs, (f) AlCl3-CDs, (g) CuCl2-CDs, and (h) FeCl3-CDs. (i) XPS spectrum and element ratios of AlCl3-CDs. (j–l) C1s, N1s, and O1s spectrum of AlCl3-CDs. (m) FTIR spectrum of AlCl3-CDs. (n) Raman spectrum of AlCl3-CDs.

PL emission spectra of (a) oPD-CDs, (b) AlCl3-CDs, (c) CuCl2-CDs, and (d) FeCl3-CDs under excitation of different wavelengths. Insets are the corresponding photographs of CDs in ethanol solution in daylight (left) and under a 365 nm lamp (right). Normalized UV–vis absorption, Ex, and Em spectra of (e) oPD-CDs, (f) AlCl3-CDs, (g) CuCl2-CDs, and (h) FeCl3-CDs. Time-resolved PL decay curves of (i) oPD-CDs, (j) AlCl3-CDs, (k) CuCl2-CDs, and (l) FeCl3-CDs.

(a) Zeta potential values of oPD-CDs, AlCl3-CDs, CuCl2-CDs, and FeCl3-CDs. (b) PLQY values of oPD-CDs, AlCl3-CDs, CuCl2-CDs, and FeCl3-CDs (c) Plot the tangent line of UV–vis spectra of yellow CDs and AlCl3-CDs to find their absorption edge. (d) Illustration of the bandgap transitions of yellow CDs and AlCl3-CDs.

(1) The possible formation process of oPD to obtain yellow CDs by self-polymerization. (2) After the introduction of AlCl3·6H2O, the polymerization process of oPD was accelerated to obtain red CDs with large sp2 conjugate domains. (3) The stable oPD-based yellow CDs can be fully converted into red CDs after the introduction of AlCl3·6H2O for the solvothermal reaction.

Fluorescence images of AlCl3-CDs in HeLa cells under different conditions. The first line is fluorescence images of HeLa cells without AlCl3-CDs (control group). The second line is fluorescence images of HeLa cells adding AlCl3-CDs (50 μg/mL).
Schematic diagram of original oPD polymerization and polymerization with electron-withdrawing catalyst.
Schematic diagram of single component red fluorescence of CDs realized by catalyst regulation.

head.ce:title: Catalyst regulation of o-phenylenediamine-based carbon dots to achieve single red emission

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Southeast University, Nanjing 211189, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Southeast University, Nanjing 211189, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Catalyst regulation of o-phenylenediamine-based carbon dots for achieving single red emission with high photoluminescence quantum yield (29.99%) and high component ratio (95.6%).

Red fluorescence is of great significance in biomedicine because of its high tissue penetration and low photodamage to organisms. While the carbon dots (CDs) obtained from o-phenylenediamine (oPD) mainly emit yellow fluorescence in a single reaction system, which greatly limits its development in long-wavelength applications. Here, we design a strategy to control the color of oPD-based CDs by only adding catalyst without changing the materials and conditions. The addition of the catalyst made the mass proportion of the obtained red CDs from less than 1% to 95.6%, and the conversion rate was up to 61.7%. The red CDs with a photoluminescence quantum yield of 29.99% have been successfully applied to live cell imaging. It is well known that catalysts can selectively change the reaction pathway and improve the reaction activity. In the process of oPD-based CD formation, we propose that the catalyst can activate the amino functional group, further accelerate the dehydrogenation and polymerization process, thus effectively increasing the sp2-conjugated domain and graphitic N content, which finally realizes the conversion of mixed CDs to single red CDs. This work provides a new idea for how to regulate the fluorescence of CDs without changing the materials and conditions in the future.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As a new kind of fluorescent nanoparticle with excellent properties, carbon dots (CDs) have made great progress in the fields of optoelectronic devices and biomedicine [1–10]. At present, the raw materials used to synthesize CDs are complex and have different structures. Among them, o-phenylenediamine (oPD) has become a popular material for the synthesis of CDs due to its conjugated structure, certain N doping, and low price. But so far, the CDs obtained from oPD are concentrated in the short-wavelength region. For example, Jiang et al.[11] used oPD to get green CDs with an emission peak at 535 nm through the solvothermal method; Gao et al.[12] took oPD as a raw material and DMF as a solvent to prepare green CDs at a high temperature of 220 ℃. Chao and co-workers used oPD as the carbon source to obtain yellow CDs by one-step hydrothermal method. With the adjustment of different polar solvents, multicolor fluorescence from cyan to orange was achieved [13]. However, it is difficult to realize the red emission of CDs in a single reaction system using oPD as raw material. This greatly limits the application and development of oPD-based CDs in biomedicine and high color rendering index white light-emitting diodes.

Some researchers have achieved red fluorescence of oPD-based CDs by adding acidic reagents or amino acid derivatives. Liu et al.[14] used oPD as a carbon source, introduced nitric acid to accelerate the oxidative process, and formed red CDs with conjugated aromatic benzene skeleton; Wang et al.[15] designed a hydrothermal synthesis route using oPD and dopamine as raw materials, and obtained red CDs with an absolute photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of 33.96%. The above work has promoted the development of oPD-based red CDs. However, the realization of red fluorescence in a single reaction system without changing the raw materials and reaction conditions needs to be further explored.

Soni and Cao et al.[16,17] have clearly pointed out that CDs synthesized from oPD are actually mixtures containing fluorescent CDs of different colors. Among them, the proportion of CDs emitting short-wavelength fluorescence is high, which makes it difficult for the obtained oPD-based CDs to display red light. Compared with short-wavelength CDs, red fluorescent CDs may have a larger size or a larger sp2-conjugated domain [18,19]. How can oPD be controlled to convert into red fluorescent CDs under the same conditions? It is difficult to achieve this goal by conventional methods. A catalyst offers the possibility for this because it can effectively change the rate and selectivity of a chemical reaction. Metal halides, as a typical Lewis acid catalyst, are widely used in organic synthesis and polymerization reactions because they can absorb electrons to promote polymerization and addition reactions. This effect may also promote the cross-linking polymerization reaction during the synthesis of oPD-based CDs. In fact, intramolecular hydrogen bond between the two adjacent amino groups of oPD make it difficult to activate, and the large steric effect between them makes it difficult to form large planar conjugate surfaces. As a result, just as shown in Scheme 1 , oPD is more likely to form a large number of yellow fluorescent 2,3-Diaminophenazine (DAP) small molecule compounds, and finally get yellow fluorescent CDs after carbonization [16]. When introducing metal halides (such as AlCl3) into the reaction system, metal cations can capture the lone pair of electrons of the amino functional groups to form complexes, which will weaken the intramolecular hydrogen bonding and steric effect of oPD, so that it can be more easily polymerized to form large conjugated domains under hydrothermal conditions. This will be more beneficial to obtain red fluorescent CDs with higher polymerization degree and larger sp2-conjugated domain. Based on this, we designed to carry out the following experiments as shown in Scheme 2 . oPD, which is not easy to obtain red fluorescence under conventional conditions, will be chosen as raw material, and AlCl3, CuCl2, and FeCl3 designed to use as catalysts to promote the polymerization of oPD to obtain the CDs with long-wavelength emission, respectively.

oPD was purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. AlCl3·6H2O and CuCl2·2H2O were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. FeCl3·6H2O was purchased from Nanjing Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Anhydrous ethanol was purchased from Wuxi Yasheng Chemical Co., Ltd. The reagents used in the experiments were all analytical grade without further purification.

Control Experiment 2: 0.6 g of oPD was fully dissolved in 30 mL of anhydrous ethanol, then placed in a 50 mL Teflon reactor and reacted in an oven at 200 ℃ for 12h. After the reaction, it was cooled to room temperature naturally, and the crude product showed yellow-green emission under a 365 nm UV lamp. At this time, 0.01786 g of AlCl3·6H2O was added to the product and placed in an oven at 200 ℃ to continue the reaction for 12h. After that, it was cooled to room temperature naturally. The obtained solution showed bright red under a 365 nm UV lamp.

First, HeLa cells were cultured in DMEM supplemented with 10% Fetal Bovine Serum (FBS) and streptomycin-penicillin (100 U/mL), and grown in an incubator (37 ℃) containing 5% CO2. Subsequently, the cytotoxic effect of AlCl3-CDs on HeLa cells was determined by CCK8 assays. The cells (8×103 cell/well) were placed in 96-well plates and grown in an incubator. After adherent for 24h, the cells were incubated with different concentrations (0.0, 50.0, 100.0, and 150.0 μg/mL) of AlCl3-CDs for another 24h. Finally, the supernatant was removed, and 100 μL of medium and 10 μL of CCK8 were added to each well away from light. The absorbance of the wells at 450 nm was recorded with the microplate reader after 2.5h of incubation.

First, the HeLa cells were seeded into a 12-well cell culture dish (8×104 cell/well) containing DMEM supplemented with 10% FBS and 5% CO2 for 24h at 37 ℃. At this time, wash cells three times to remove the nonadherent cells via PBS (10 mM, pH 7.4). Then the cells were incubated in DMEM combined with 50 μg/mL AlCl3-CDs for 60 min. The HeLa cells were washed with PBS three times before imaging. Finally, the fluorescence images of cells were observed with Zeiss Ism880 confocal laser scanning microscope.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were obtained by a Talos F200X microscope. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was conducted on a Shimadzu/Kratos AXIS Ultra DLD instrument. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recognized with a Nicolet 5700 spectrometer. Raman spectra were gathered using a Thermo Scientific DXR spectrometer at an excitation wavelength of 532 nm. Zeta potential was measured by a Dispersion Technology Instrument DT310. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu UV-2600 spectrometer. PL spectra were recorded on a SHIMADZU RF-6000 fluorescence spectrometer. The absolute PLQY and time-resolved PL spectra were recorded by a PluoroLog 3-TCSPC steady transient fluorescence spectrometer equipped with the integrating sphere. Inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) analysis was conducted on an Agilent 7800 instrument.

It can be seen from Fig. 1 that in the absence of a catalyst, the CDs obtained from oPD as raw materials are mainly yellow, and the mass proportion and conversion rate of red components is pitifully small, only less than 1%. After adding the catalyst, the proportion of red CDs has been significantly increased, especially when AlCl3 is used as the catalyst, the obtained CDs are basically red, accounting for as high as 95.6%. The conversion rate of red CDs to oPD also increases from less than 1% to 61.7% (The calculation method of the conversion rate and mass proportion is detailed in the synthesis method section). CuCl2 and FeCl3 also have an obvious promotion effect, and the mass proportion of red CDs increases to 21% and 19%, and the conversion rates are 11.8% and 10.2%. To compare the morphology, structure, and charge of the obtained red CDs by different methods, we purified them through silica gel column chromatography, and named oPD-CDs, AlCl3-CDs, CuCl2-CDs, and FeCl3-CDs, respectively. From Fig. 2
(a)–(d), four kinds of CDs are quasi-spherical with good dispersion, and their average particle sizes are 6.45±0.1, 6.99±0.2, 6.05±0.2, and 5.65±0.1 nm (Fig. 2 (e)–(h)). The obvious lattice fringes in HRTEM images indicate that all CDs have good crystallinity, and the spacing of lattice fringes of 0.21 nm corresponds to the (100) crystal plane of graphite [20,21]. The zeta potentials of red CDs are −6.67, −6.50, −6.57, and −6.53 mV (Fig. 4 (a) and
Fig. S5 ), suggesting the CDs are negatively charged. The zeta potential values in the same range (both 6.5–6.7 mV) hint that the catalysts are not doped into the structure of red CDs [22–25]. We also compared the particle size and crystallinity of yellow CDs obtained without adding a catalyst. As can be seen from Fig. S6 (a), the average particle size of yellow CDs is 6.97±0.4 nm, which is similar to the red CDs, indicating that the conversion from yellow to red fluorescence is not caused by the quantum size effect. No obvious lattice fringes were found in the yellow CDs, proving that the crystallinity is worse than the red CDs.

In order to explore the structure, surface composition, and degree of graphitization, AlCl3-CDs was selected as the representative for the relevant characterization. The XPS spectrum contains C, N, and O, which account for 81.91%, 8.07%, and 10.02% (Fig. 2 (i) and
Table S1 ). There is no aluminum peak in the spectrum, proving that the purification method can remove a small amount of aluminum ions and the AlCl3 does not dope into the conjugated CDs. There are three peaks in the C1s spectra, and the proportion of C=C/C=N content is the highest (91.79%) (Fig. 2 (j) and
Table S2 ), compared with the C=C/C=N content (82.41%) of yellow CDs (Fig. S6 (c) and
Table S2 ), it is sufficient to prove that AlCl3-CDs have a high sp2-conjugated structure [11,13,16,18]. The peaks in the N1s spectra correspond to pyridinic N, amino N, and graphitic N (Fig. 2 (k) and Fig. S6 (d)) [26,27]. According to the comparison of different N-binding forms, it can be found that during the conversion process from yellow to red CDs, the graphitic N content is significantly increased, indicating that the graphitic N content is also an influencing factor of PL redshift ( Table S2 ). The peaks in the O1s spectra correspond to C=O and C-O (Fig. 2 (l)) [28], showing the CDs contained groups such as hydroxyl. In the FTIR spectrum, the C=C bond (1519 cm−1) has a relatively strong signal, attributed to a conjugated carbon structure (Fig. 2 (m)). The existence of a C-N bond (1220 cm−1) means the form of a N heterocyclic ring [29–31]. Similarly, no signal of aluminum ions was detected in the spectrum. Subsequently, we conducted ICP-MS characterization, and the results further proved that there were no aluminum ions in the internal structure of AlCl3-CDs ( Fig. S7 ). The experimental results are consistent with the analysis of zeta potential, XPS, and FTIR spectra. In addition, the two typical Raman characteristic peaks are located at 1376 and 1529 cm−1, respectively. The intensity ratio of the G band to D band is 1.36, while the IG/ID of yellow CDs is 1.11, suggesting that AlCl3-CDs have a higher degree of graphitization than yellow CDs (Fig. 2 (n) and Fig. S6 (f)) [32,33]. The above structural characterization fully demonstrates that AlCl3-CDs have a larger sp2-conjugated domain and higher crystallinity, which is beneficial for realizing long-wavelength emission.

To further investigate the optical performance of the red CDs, PL spectra, UV–vis absorption spectra, PL lifetimes, and PLQY were characterized. From Fig. 3
(a)–(d), two main fluorescence peaks located at 600 and 650 nm, and a shoulder peak at 710 nm, show excitation-independent characteristics. We imported the PL spectra into the color coordinate calculation program ( Fig. S8 ). The results show that the CIE color coordinates of oPD-CDs are (0.63, 0.37), and the others are (0.64, 0.36). All coordinates belong to the red emission area. The normalized PL spectra under optimal excitation show that the curves and peaks of red CDs coincide well ( Fig. S9 ), indicating the red CDs obtained by different methods have the same fluorescence source. Compared with the PL spectra of yellow CDs (Fig. S4 (b)), the emission peak of red CDs obtained by adding a catalyst is red-shifted by 50 nm. Fig. 3 (e)–(h) are the normalized profiles of UV–vis, Ex, and Em spectra. The CDs have strong absorption peaks in the range of 200–280 nm, which is mainly attributed to the π-π* transition of the C=C bond in the aromatic rings. In the range of 500–600 nm, the absorption peaks of oPD-CDs are relatively weak, and the others are relatively strong, verifying the red CDs all have a certain sp2-conjugated structure, the peaks red-shift by about 100 nm compared to yellow CDs (Fig. S4 (e)), which means a larger conjugation system has been formed after adding the catalyst [18,34,35]. In addition, the Ex spectra coincide with the absorption peak in the 500–600 nm region, suggesting the emission may originate from the carbon core and has bandgap emission properties [36,37]. The overlap between the Em and absorption spectra is small, which can effectively avoid self-absorption and is beneficial for achieving strong fluorescence emission [38,39]. From Fig. 3 (i)–(l), all curves fitted by mono-exponential functions, proving the emission is dominated by the carbon core, and the fluorescence is mainly derived from the sp2-conjugated structure [36,40]. The lifetimes have similar decay trends, indicating the transitions originate from the same excited state ( Table S3 ). The catalytic effect of the AlCl3 increases the PLQY value from 11.94% to 29.99% (Fig. 4 (b) and
Fig. S10 ); this is probably because as the sp2 conjugate domain increased, the content of defect states will be relatively reduced, effectively inhibiting the non-radiative pathways and enhancing radiative recombination efficiency.

The above data analysis shows that red CDs have similar properties, but there is a big difference in the conversion rate of red fluorescence. Choosing AlCl3 as the catalyst can efficiently increase the production of red CDs, otherwise most of the CDs are yellow. The comparison of the particle size, lattice fringes, C=C/C=N and graphitic N content, Raman spectra, and UV–vis absorption peaks of yellow and red CDs shows that the conversion from yellow to red fluorescence may be caused by the increased sp2-conjugated domain and graphitic N content. Based on this, we speculated the formation process and mechanism of red CDs. First, oPD will react slowly to form DAP at a high temperature without a catalyst (Fig. 5
(1)). As the prolongation of the reaction time, the dehydrogenation process will occur between the DAP molecules to form larger conjugated aromatic polymers [18,41]. During the reaction process, the participation of solvent ethanol makes the polymer edge bonded C=O and O–H groups. With the intensification of the polymerization and carbonization process, a large number of yellow CDs are gradually formed [16]. Due to the limited degree of polymerization, the final sp2 conjugate structure is small, which cannot effectively form red CDs. Subsequently, after introducing the electron-absorbing catalyst, it can activate the amino functional group and enhance the solvothermal reaction activity by weakening the intramolecular hydrogen bond and steric hindrance effect by constructing complexes with the amino group of oPD. Thus, oPD molecules are easier to polymerize and form conjugated coordination compounds and further polymerize to form large-size polymers at high temperatures (Scheme 1 and Fig. 5 (2)) [28,31]. With the prolongation of reaction time, the process of dehydrogenation and in-situ carbonization occur between the polymers. Finally, the carbon core with a larger sp2 conjugated size is formed [19,42]. AlCl3-CDs have the highest proportion of red fluorescence, indicating that the catalyst AlCl3 has relatively higher catalytic activity than CuCl2 and FeCl3. Since the catalyst can promote cross-linking polymerization and increase the conjugated domain, can the short-wavelength CDs obtained by conventional methods be further promoted to obtain red CDs by adding a catalyst? To this end, we carried out the following experiment: First, adding an appropriate amount of AlCl3 to the obtained short-wavelength CDs mixture. After the reaction, almost all the yellow CDs were converted into red fluorescence (Fig. 5 (3)). This experiment further proved that the catalyst can promote the cross-linking and polymerization of CDs, achieve a larger conjugated carbon core, and obtain long-wavelength fluorescent CDs. Furthermore, we calculated the band gaps between the HOMO and LOMO energy levels. Made tangent lines along the abrupt point of the UV–vis absorption spectra, and the absorption edges (λedge) of yellow and red emission CDs are determined to be 492 and 603 nm (Fig. 4 (c)). The corresponding energy gap is calculated as 2.52 and 2.06 eV according to the equation Egap=1240/λedge [43,44]; it can be found that the decreasing energy gap is consistent with their progressively redshifted spectra (Fig. 4 (d)). The above shows that under the action of high temperature and catalyst, the sp2-conjugated domain of the carbon core increases gradually, which realizes the fluorescence conversion process of CDs from yellow to red emission.

In this study, the preparation of red CDs using oPD as raw material was innovatively achieved by only introducing a catalyst, which prompted the oPD molecules to form conjugated structure coordination compounds and accelerated the dehydrogenation and polymerization process, further made the sp2 conjugate domain and graphitic N content increase and the energy gap decrease continuously, realizing the conversion of mixed CDs to single red CDs. When AlCl3 was used as a catalyst, the mass proportion of red CDs in the mixed CDs increased from less than 1% to 95.6%, the conversion rate of red CDs to oPD was up to 61.7%, and the absolute PLQY value increased from 11.94% to 29.99%. Furthermore, we stained HeLa cells and fully demonstrated the potential of AlCl3-CDs as a fluorescent probe for live cell imaging. We also used CuCl2 and FeCl3 as catalysts to convert oPD-based CDs to single red emission, confirming the universality of catalysts to regulate the PL characteristics of CDs. This work not only effectively confirms that the catalyst can selectively regulate CDs to obtain a single component fluorescence but also provides a new idea for realizing the red/near-infrared emission of oPD-based CDs without changing the raw materials and reaction conditions.
The manuscript was written with contributions from all authors. All the authors approved the final version of the manuscript.

Pinyi He: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Jianliang Bai: Supervision, Formal analysis. Fu Qin: Software, Resources. Xinyu Wang: Supervision, Formal analysis. Xu Yu: Resources, Investigation. Yongkang Yao: Resources. Lili Ren: Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22275035).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2024.159367.

The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
